summary: Joel discusses several topics: seasonal affective disorder as a likely cause of programmer malaise, a usability bug in Google Suggest, and a court ruling on competitor advertising via Google search. His central argument is that Google Suggest, despite its bugs, represents a paradigm shift in web user expectations — users will soon demand responsive, AJAX-style interfaces instead of tolerating full-page reloads. He frames this as 'raising the bar' for all web applications.
key_points: Programmer dissatisfaction with their work may correlate more with seasonal affective disorder than actual problems with the profession.
Google Suggest has a usability bug where the popup dropdown intercepts clicks intended for the search button, violating user expectations.
Google Suggest's real importance isn't search — it's training users to expect highly responsive, interactive web interfaces.
Web applications that rely on full-page reloads for basic interactions like sorting, zooming, or deleting will no longer be acceptable once users experience responsive alternatives.
Competitors advertising on your brand name in Google search is legal but tacky.
money_quotes: Install some bright lights, drink some coffee, take a vacation in Tahiti, and tell me in April if you still think software development is depressing.
It's important not for searching, but because it's going to teach web users to expect highly responsive user interfaces.
They will no longer tolerate the full-page-reload-and-scroll-to-the-top that Mapquest has conditioned them to accept.
If you have an email application, and you show people a list of email and give them a button to delete email as spam, they're going to expect virtually instantaneous response time.
That's what I meant by 'raising the bar.'
You don't see Wal*Mart advertising when you search for Tiffany.
key_insight: Google Suggest's lasting impact isn't better search — it's permanently raising user expectations for responsive, interactive web interfaces and making full-page reloads unacceptable.
